coredata.dc:title: Influence of precursor chemistry in the property of carbon nanodots and its application for the degradation of methyl orange
coredata.dc:creator: Leuterio, Jessa C.
Camacho, Drexel H.
Bantang, Jose Paolo O.
Argamino, Cristian Ryan A.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon nanodots (CNDs) are interesting materials from renewable sources that exhibit unique fluorescence property. This study reports that sulfated carrageenan (ᴋ-, ɩ-, λ-) as carbon precursors for CNDs impart unique properties not observed in a non-sulfated source. Hydrothermal process afforded CNDs having quasi-spherical shapes (TEM), nano sizes (TEM: 3.8 nm; DLS 1.0–2.3 nm), luminous green property under UV light, and presence of sulfur in CNDs (EDS, FTIR). The CNDs were embedded in a carrageenan film matrix to prevent its aggregation and to improve its stability affording brown-colored free-standing films that have better stability (zeta potential) compared to bare CNDs in solution. The CNDs from carrageenan exhibited unique acidic property in water, which is not observed in CNDs from galactose indicating that the sulfate-laden carrageenan precursor imparts unique property to the CNDs. Degradation activity on methyl orange (MO) dye was only observed for CNDs derived from carrageenan and not from galactose confirming the critical role of precursor chemistry in imparting unique acidic properties to the resulting CNDs. The study demonstrated the difference in the carrageenan-based CNDs compared to those prepared from simple sugar and showed its application for the degradation of dyes without the need for direct exposure to sunlight.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Carrageenan
Film composite
Sensing
Methyl orange

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the film-based carbon nanodots
Characterization of CNDs in solution and in the film
Investigation on the effects of CNDs on dyes
Characterization of carbon nanodots (CNDs) and CND-films
Activity of CNDs for the degradation of methyl orange (MO) dye

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Funding: This work was supported by the Philippine Department of Science and Technology-Science Education Institute (DOST-SEI) through the Accelerated Science and Technology Human Resource Development (ASTHRDP) program for the graduate scholarship grant to J. C. Leuterio. We acknowledge De La Salle University-Central Instrumentation Facility (DLSU-CIF) for the Graduate Research Residency Program support to J. C. Leuterio. The DLSU-CIF NMR and Spectroscopy laboratories are acknowledged for the use of instruments. We thank Ms. May Bernardo of UP Manila for her valuable help in the Fluorescence analysis. We wish to acknowledge Virgilio Ebajo, Jr., Jude Rolan dela Cruz, Alona Intac, and Deanne Hazel Pelobello of DLSU-CIF for assistance in conducting the experiments.

Funding: This work was supported by the Philippine Department of Science and Technology-Science Education Institute (DOST-SEI) through the Accelerated Science and Technology Human Resource Development (ASTHRDP) program for the graduate scholarship grant to J. C. Leuterio. We acknowledge De La Salle University-Central Instrumentation Facility (DLSU-CIF) for the Graduate Research Residency Program support to J. C. Leuterio. The DLSU-CIF NMR and Spectroscopy laboratories are acknowledged for the use of instruments. We thank Ms. May Bernardo of UP Manila for her valuable help in the Fluorescence analysis. We wish to acknowledge Virgilio Ebajo, Jr. Jude Rolan dela Cruz, Alona Intac, and Deanne Hazel Pelobello of DLSU-CIF for assistance in conducting the experiments.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A-B) Digital images of aqueous solution of CNDs from left to right: ᴋ-carr CNDs, ɩ-carr CNDs, λ-carr CNDs and Gal CNDs under (A) daylight and (B) UV light. (C-D) Digital images of CND-loaded films from left to right: ᴋ-carr-CND film, ɩ-carr-CND film, λ-carr-CND film, Gal-CND film, and carrageenan film without CND (control) under (C) daylight and (D) UV light.

(A) TEM image of ᴋ-carr-CNDs and its (B) particle size distribution. Hydrodynamic sizes of (C) raw CNDs and (D) CNDs in films. Insets: Illustration of hydrodynamic diameter (Dh) of CNDs in (C) the absence and (D) in the presence of ᴋ-carrageenan film matrix.
UV spectra of (A) aqueous raw CNDs and (B) CND films dissolved in water.

(A) UV spectra of blank methyl orange (MO) and MO with ɩ-carr CNDs, Inset: observation before and after addition of ɩ-carr CNDs; (B) Classical chemistry of protonation of MO accompanied by color change and red shift in UV absorbance. (C) Time-dependent absorption measurement of MO degradation in the presence of ɩ-carr CNDs at 3.0 mg/L MO concentration. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

1H NMR spectra of (A) pure methyl orange (MO), (B) ɩ-carr CNDs, (C) MO with ɩ-carr CNDs at t = 30 min, and (D) MO with ɩ-carr CNDs at t = 60 min. Inset: structure of MO with NMR signal assignments. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Pseudo-first order kinetics of the carr-CND-films in the degradation of Methyl orange (MO). Inset shows the rate constants. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
pH and UV absorbances of MO solution in the presence of different CND films.

Absorbance a of MO solution at t = 35 min
MO with Carrageenan film without CNDs (control)

head.ce:title: Influence of precursor chemistry in the property of carbon nanodots and its application for the degradation of methyl orange

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Organic Materials and Interfaces Unit, CENSER, De La Salle University, 2401 Taft Avenue, Manila, 0922, Philippines
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Organic Materials and Interfaces Unit, CENSER, De La Salle University, 2401 Taft Avenue, Manila, 0922, Philippines

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Chemistry Department, De La Salle University, 2401 Taft Avenue, Manila, 0922, Philippines.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon nanodots (CNDs) are interesting materials from renewable sources that exhibit unique fluorescence property. This study reports that sulfated carrageenan (ᴋ-, ɩ-, λ-) as carbon precursors for CNDs impart unique properties not observed in a non-sulfated source. Hydrothermal process afforded CNDs having quasi-spherical shapes (TEM), nano sizes (TEM: 3.8 nm; DLS 1.0–2.3 nm), luminous green property under UV light, and presence of sulfur in CNDs (EDS, FTIR). The CNDs were embedded in a carrageenan film matrix to prevent its aggregation and to improve its stability affording brown-colored free-standing films that have better stability (zeta potential) compared to bare CNDs in solution. The CNDs from carrageenan exhibited unique acidic property in water, which is not observed in CNDs from galactose indicating that the sulfate-laden carrageenan precursor imparts unique property to the CNDs. Degradation activity on methyl orange (MO) dye was only observed for CNDs derived from carrageenan and not from galactose confirming the critical role of precursor chemistry in imparting unique acidic properties to the resulting CNDs. The study demonstrated the difference in the carrageenan-based CNDs compared to those prepared from simple sugar and showed its application for the degradation of dyes without the need for direct exposure to sunlight.

ce:section.ce:para: Carrageenan is a sulfated polysaccharide derived from edible red seaweeds. The three principal types of carrageenans are kappa (ᴋ), iota (ɩ), and lambda (λ), which vary from one another by the amount and position of sulfate ester groups in their galactose dimer [1]. These biomolecules are widely used in processed food applications owing to its gelling, thickening, and emulsifying properties. However, Tobacman [2] reported on the harmful gastrointestinal effects of carrageenan in animals, which indicate that both degraded and undegraded carrageenans are associated with the development of gastrointestinal malignancy and inflammatory bowel disease. Thus, calls have been mounted to consider the prohibition of carrageenan as a food additive. If this happens, the seaweed and carrageenan industry worldwide would be in a serious problem. Thus, there is an urgent need to explore non-food applications and alternative uses of carrageenan to help the seaweed farmers and the carrageenan industry.

Carbon nanodots (CNDs) is a promising carbon nanomaterial that has been of interest in the last decade. Various applications have been developed within a short period due to its intense fluorescence, water-solubility, photostability, functionalizability, good chemical stability, and low toxicity [3,4]. The preparation of CNDs includes the top-down approach where bulk materials are converted into CNDs, but the process requires expensive equipment, exposure to harsh chemicals, and usage of toxic reagents. As an alternative, the bottom-up approach involves assembling atoms to form CNDs, which makes use of small-scale starting material like carbohydrates under heating, ultrasounds, or microwave conditions [5]. The hydrothermal method is another technique that uses water and the precursor inside a sealed container heated above the boiling point of water. The high pressure inside degrades the precursor without releasing CO2 and allows the assembly of carbon atoms to form CNDs. The technique is simple, uses low energy, and is an eco-friendly way of transforming available materials into useful products. Biomasses such as onion [6], lemon peel [7], ginger [8], and egg white [9] have been converted into CNDs using the hydrothermal method. A cursory survey of the literature showed the absence of reports comparing the 3 types of carrageenans as starting materials for CNDs, hence this work.

Synthetic azo dyes are used in large quantities in textile, paint, and cosmetic industries that contribute to the health and environmental issues [10]. Azo dyes are recognized as potential genotoxic, mutagenic, and carcinogenic and have been demonstrated to have toxic effects on aquatic animals, humans, and microorganisms [11]. The lethal dose (LD50) of methyl orange (MO) dye is at 60 mg/kg. A wide range of treatment methods has been used for wastewater polluted with azo dyes, such as photocatalytic degradation [12], electrocoagulation [13], adsorption [14], ozonation [15], and microbial process [16]. There is still a need to develop new systems to degrade azo dyes under mild conditions. A simple system that is portable and can be used readily on-field is highly desirable.

This work explores the utilization of three carrageenan sources (ᴋ-, ɩ-, λ-) as precursors for the synthesis of CNDs. Since it is a sulfated polysaccharide, this work surmises that the sulfur in carrageenan can be incorporated in the CND structure imparting unique properties. The utilization of sulfate-containing polysaccharides to prepare CNDs are interesting because this functional group may play an important role in its degradation activity especially on environmentally polluting dyes. It is the objective of this work to synthesize CNDs from carrageenan (ᴋ-, ɩ-, λ-) using the hydrothermal method, fabricate a film-based CNDs, and explore the potential degradation activity of the CNDs towards MO dye.

All chemicals were used as received. Food grade κ-, ι-, and λ-carrageenans were obtained from MIOKA biosystems. Galactose was obtained from Sigma Aldrich. Analytical grade MO dye was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. The Teflon-coated stainless autoclave was used as a reaction chamber for the hydrothermal method. Syringe filters (Whatman; 0.2 μm PVDF) were used to purify the CNDs. A freeze dryer (Christ Gamma 2–16 LSCPlus) was used to dry CNDs. The pH value was measured using a pH meter (Jenco Model 6175).

The general procedure for the preparation of CNDs involves dispersing the κ-carrageenan (2.0 g) in 40 ml distilled water by stirring continuously for 15 min. The mixture was agitated in an ultrasonicator bath for 20 min and transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless autoclave (100 ml). The sealed autoclave was placed in a drying oven at 200 °C for 4 h. The mixture was cooled to room temperature and was subjected to centrifugation at 15,000 rpm for 20 min to separate the black solid particles (hydrochar) from the mixture. The obtained yellowish-brown solution was collected and purified using a 0.2 μm PVDF syringe filter (Whatman). The same process was used to prepare CNDs from ι-carrageenan, λ-carrageenan, and galactose (control). The resulting raw CNDs are referred to henceforth as κ-carr CNDs, ι-carr CNDs, λ-carr CNDs, and Gal-CNDs for CNDs prepared from κ-, ι-, λ-carrageenans, and galactose, respectively. Some CNDs were freeze-dried to afford sticky black powders that are hygroscopic, while the rest remain in as-is solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the film-based carbon nanodots
ce:section.ce:para: Thin films containing CNDs were prepared using a uniform film matrix. The raw CNDs in aqueous solution (10 ml) were stirred thoroughly with 1% aqueous κ-carrageenan (100 ml) and was heated at 45 °C for 15 min. While hot, a fixed volume (35 ml) of the mixture was poured into flat plastic polypropylene Petri dishes to achieve a uniform volume. The mixture was cooled until it formed a gel and was allowed to dry at room temperature to afford a free-standing thin film. The films were cut into square (2″x 2″) sizes. For comparison, a κ-carrageenan film without CNDs was prepared separately as control. The resulting films are referred to henceforth as κ-carr-CND film, ι-carr-CND film, λ-carr-CND film, and Gal-CND film for films that contains CNDs prepared from κ-, ι-, λ-carrageenans, and galactose, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CNDs in solution and in the film
ce:section.ce:para: Absorption spectra of the raw CNDs in aqueous solutions were recorded using a UV–Vis spectrometer (UV-2600 Shimadzu) at wavelength scan in the range from 200 to 700 nm. A fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu RF-6000) was employed to record fluorescence spectra of the raw CNDs in solutions and the CND-loaded films dissolved in water. Hydrodynamic size and zeta potential of the raw CNDs and CND films in water solutions were measured using Dynamic Light Scattering (Malvern, Zetasizer). The size and morphology of freeze-dried CNDs were performed using Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (JEOL SM-IT500HR) and Transmission Electron Microscope (JEOL 2010F). The FTIR spectra of the freeze-dried CNDs and the CND films were obtained using an FTIR spectrophotometer (Agilent Cary 630) by ATR method. NMR analysis (JEOL ECZ600) was performed for freeze-dried CNDs. 1H NMR analysis was performed in 64 scans at +26 °C in D2O while 13C NMR analysis was performed in 1024 scans at +26 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation on the effects of CNDs on dyes
ce:section.ce:para:
CND films, cut in 2″x 2″ sizes, were investigated for their interaction with MO dye. In brief, the ι-carr-CND film was immersed in 50 ml aqueous solutions of MO. The pH of the dye solution was adjusted to the desired value by the addition of 0.1 M HCl or 5.0 M NaOH solution and the pH value of the mixture was measured by pH meter. The mixture was left for continuous mild stirring. After definite intervals of time, an aliquot of the solution was transferred to 1.5 ml centrifuge tubes and centrifuged for 2 min at 15,000 rpm to stop the degradation reaction by precipitating the CND films and the liquid was transferred to a quartz cuvette. The results of the degradation process were observed by measuring the decrease in the absorbance spectrum of dyes by UV–Vis spectrometer over a wavelength range of 200–800 nm. Using the optimized film size, the catalytic activity was compared with the other CND films from different sources. The effectivity of the best CND film as a catalyst was compared with the catalytic activity of the aqueous solution of the raw CNDs from the same source. The extent of dye degradation was calculated using the relation:
Degradation% = [(Ao − At)/Ao] × 100%
where Ao is the initial absorbance of dye solution and At is the absorbance at different time intervals.

Carbon nanodots (CNDs) were prepared from three different carrageenan sources (ᴋ-, ɩ, λ). CNDs from galactose were also prepared as the control because it is the main sugar component in carrageenan but without the sulfate ester moieties. The obtained CNDs in aqueous dispersion is a clear yellow-brown solution under daylight conditions (Fig. 1 A), but turned luminous green color when viewed under UV (Fig. 1B) signifying the successful conversion of the precursors into CNDs with good dispersibility. The synthesis of CNDs in the hydrothermal method usually goes through the pyrolysis of carbonaceous precursors at high temperatures, which results in carbonization and nucleation [17]. Carrageenan is a carbohydrate that contains sulfated galactans that satisfies the C, H, O ratio of 1:2:1 where C and O exist in the form that allows dehydration under hydrothermal conditions; thus, it is an ideal biomaterial for the synthesis of CNDs [18].

The as-is solutions of CNDs (raw) or the redissolved dry CNDs tend to agglomerate over time losing their unique optical properties. To prevent the CNDs from agglomeration, the CNDs were incorporated in a film. Films prepared from 1% κ-carrageenan afforded a free-standing stable film where nanoparticles such as AgNPs have previously been incorporated [19]. The dried film is soluble in water allowing the storing of the nanoparticle in the film and releasing it once dissolved. Films containing the CNDs afforded brown-colored thin films (Fig. 1C). The control film with no CNDs afforded a white-colored opaque thin film. Viewed under UV light, the films containing CNDs showed the characteristic luminous property while the control showed white color (Fig. 1D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of carbon nanodots (CNDs) and CND-films
ce:section.ce:para: The SEM morphologies of the freeze-dried raw CNDs showed quasi-spherical particles with non-uniform sizes. Analysis of the ᴋ-carr-CNDs using TEM (Fig. 2 A and B) confirms the quasi-spherical shapes with an average size of 3.8 nm ± 1.3 nm typical for CND particles. CNDs tend to aggregate in some portion, which can be attributed to processing conditions. Hydrodynamic sizes (Dh) of the raw CNDs measured in the presence of water using Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) show that the average Dh of the raw CNDs (Fig. 2C) is in the range 1.7–3.0 nm (ᴋ-carr CNDs = 1.7 ± 0.77 nm; ɩ-carr CNDs = 2.3 ± 1.62 nm; λ-carr CNDs = 1.0 ± 0.37 nm; and Gal CNDs = 3.0 ± 0.42 nm). The presence of the film matrix increased the Dh of CNDs (Figure 2D) to 261–444 nm range (ᴋ-carr-CND film = 263 ± 215.4 nm; ɩ-carr-CND film = 261 ± 207.4 nm; λ-carr-CND film = 320 ± 37.9 nm; and Gal-CND film = 444 ± 72.08 nm). DLS determines the Dh of a particle by measuring the scattering light intensity fluctuation of the sample solution caused by the Brownian motion of the particles in the solution [20]. Dh depends on several properties such as polarity and charges as well as on the size and shape of the nanomaterial. These properties are attributed to the electrostatic interactions in the system [21]. Results show that the Dh of the CNDs in the film is bigger in comparison to the raw CNDs, which is expected due to the presence of the polysaccharide carrageenan matrix. The surface of CNDs interacts not only with the water solvent but also with the polymeric film matrix dissolved in water. Strong hydration occurred between the electrical charges on the CND surface with the ᴋ-carrageenan since it is negatively charged due to the sulfate groups attached to it, which leads to a great charge density all over the surface of CNDs (Fig. 2D inset).

The Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy (EDS) result indicates that the atoms present in the carrageenan precursor were retained upon its conversion to CNDs. The measured mass percentage of sulfur observed in ᴋ-carr CNDs, ɩ-carr CNDs, and λ-carr CNDs were 3.53 ± 0.16%, 4.19 ± 0.19%, and 5.29 ± 0.14%, respectively, which is consistent with the increasing trend since the ᴋ-, ɩ-, and λ-carrageenans contain 1, 2, and 3 sulfate ester groups per disaccharide unit, respectively. The observation is confirmed by the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) analysis (Figs. S1 and S2) where the functional groups present in the precursor [22] were retained in the corresponding CNDs such as the O-H band (3343 cm−1), C-H stretching (2900 cm−1), and C-O stretching (1022 cm−1), O=S=O symmetric vibration (1189 cm−1) and galactose-sulfate stretching (870 cm−1). The peaks at 1660 cm−1 and 1518 cm−1 reveals the presence of C=O and C=C (aromatic) bonds, respectively, which are not visible in the raw carrageenan. The 1H NMR scan of the CNDs (Fig. S3A) showed aromatic protons (δ 6.5–8.0 ppm), benzylic protons (δ 2.0–5.0 ppm) and protons in the environment of hydroxyl, ether, or carbonyl groups. The 13C NMR (Fig. S3B) spectrum of the CNDs confirms the presence of sp2- (δ 90–180 ppm) and sp3- (δ 8–80 ppm) hybridized carbon atoms [8] as well as carbonyl carbons (δ 170–185 ppm) in the CNDs. These results show the presence of an aromatic structure, abundant oxygen-containing groups, and sulfur moieties on the surface of carrageenan-based CNDs. The results are consistent with the recent report by Wang et al. (2021) where X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of carrageenan-derived carbon nanodots prepared using the similar hydrothermal process, revealed the presence of C, O, and S peaks indicating that the modifications on the surface of the CNDs contain oxygen and sulfur moieties [34].

The UV–vis absorption spectra of the raw CNDs in aqueous solutions (Fig. 3 A) showed a weak π-π* transition (∼230 nm) due to π conjugated aromatic system and a strong n-π* transition (282 nm) attributed the carbonyl and other oxygen-containing compounds that is very prevalent in carbon nanodots [23] consistent with the reported UV absorption from the literature [24,25]. The effect of the carrageenan film matrix on the absorption spectra of the CNDs (Fig. 3B) showed a shift to a shorter wavelength (from 282 nm to ∼250 nm) indicative of a decrease or removal of conjugation, which can be triggered by a change in polarity of the solvent. An acidic solution can turn a chromophore to undergo blue shift (change to shorter wavelength) since the H+ ions can react with an electron pair in a functional group (auxochrome) attached to the periphery of the CND carbon backbone making it unavailable for conjugation [26]. The pH values for raw CNDs in aqueous solutions were found to be acidic in the range of 1.86–2.34 (Table 1 column 2) and the pH values of the aqueous solutions of carr-CND films were found to be in the range 3.24–3.67 (Table 1 column 3). For comparison, the pH of raw carrageenan film dissolved in water is 5.0, while that of Gal-CND is 4.46 confirming that the CNDs derived from carrageenans are more acidic. It can also be observed that λ-carr-CND have the lowest pH value among the carr-CNDs showing that the degree of sulfation in CNDs has a role in the acidity property of CNDs.

The spectrofluorometric analysis revealed the fluorescence characteristics of the CNDs in an aqueous medium showing the λex dependence of the emission wavelength and intensity (Fig. S4). Results show that a red shift was observed as the excitation wavelengths increase from 320 to 420 nm accompanied by a decrease in fluorescence intensity. The emission spectra of ᴋ-carr and ɩ-carr CNDs (Figs. S4A–B) showed strong emission peak centered at 435 nm when excited at 340 nm while the λ-carr and Gal CNDs (Figs. S4C–D) showed strong emission peaks at 425 nm when excited at 320 nm confirming the fluorescent nature of the raw CNDs. The effect of the carrageenan film matrix in the fluorescence property of CND films dissolved in water (Figure S4A1-D1) also showed a red shift but a noticeable decrease in fluorescence intensity was observed compared to the raw CND solutions. The films containing carr-CNDs showed strong emission peaks centered at 400 nm when excited at 320 nm, while the Gal-CND film showed an emission peak at 420 when excited at 320 nm. Various reports have prepared fluorescent CNDs through different precursors and synthetic methods yet the photoluminescence mechanism of CNDs remains unsettled and required further research. Nevertheless, the red shift in the emission spectra may be associated with the inhomogeneous size distribution of CNDs along with the presence of different surface states created by numerous functional groups such as C-O, C=O, and O=C-OH [27]. When CNDs are excited by a photon of a specific wavelength, these surface traps prevail but when they are excited with different wavelengths other types of emission traps dominate, which results in λex dependence of CNDs. The differences in the maximum fluorescence intensity of the CNDs are attributed to the sensitivity of the intensity to the concentration of CNDs, thus, the amount of water added to CNDs is an important factor for fluorescence intensity. Diluting CNDs in deionized water lessens collision extinguishment and self-absorption in high concentrations [28].

The zeta potential measurements of raw CNDs show negative surface potentials with charge densities in the range −7.13 to −29 mV (ᴋ-carr CNDs = −29 ± 5.16; ɩ-carr CNDs = −7.50 ± 5.69; λ-carr CNDs = −27.3 ± 7.15; Gal CNDs = −7.13 ± 5.21 mV). Zeta potentials determine the long-term stability of nanoparticles where zeta values between −10mV and +10 mV are not stable and usually undergo rapid agglomeration. This was generally observed in raw CNDs where agglomerations were observed over time. The effect of the film matrix in the zeta potential of CNDs afforded more negative surface potentials in the range −25.2 to −56 mV (ᴋ-carr-CND film = −25.5 ± 4.51; ɩ-carr-CND film = −28.5 ± 5.09; λ-carr-CND film = −34.3 ± 5.01; Gal-CND film = −56.0 ± 2.97). A dramatic increase in the magnitude of the zeta potential was observed in ɩ-carr-CNDs and in Gal-CNDs indicating changes in the surface chemistry imparted by the polysaccharide matrix interacting with the CNDs in an aqueous medium. Nanoparticles that have sufficiently high zeta potentials of the same sign will not agglomerate due to repulsive electrostatic forces between particles with like charges. The measured zeta potentials of CNDs in the presence of the polymeric carrageenan chains impart stability to the CNDs. Thus, storing the CNDs in a film matrix and dissolving them in water to release the CNDs where the polymeric matrix helps in its long-term stability, offers a very practical solution to the CND agglomeration problem that would widen the applicability of CNDs in various applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Activity of CNDs for the degradation of methyl orange (MO) dye
ce:section.ce:para: The raw CNDs were explored for the degradation of MO dye. The UV–vis spectrum of pure MO (Fig. 4 A, Table 2 entry 1) in water showed an absorption peak at 426 nm reflective of its yellow color and attributed to the conjugated system in MO. When raw ɩ-carr CNDs were added, the mixture showed a peak at ∼282 nm attributed to the ɩ-carr-CNDs and a peak at 523 nm (Fig. 4A, Table 2 entry 2). The latter peak is due to the MO that has undergone a red shift from 426 to 523 nm. As the absorbance peak of MO shifts to a longer wavelength, higher absorbance was observed. A notable observation was the change in the pH of the samples. Initially, the measured pH of pure MO is 6.32 and the pH lowered to 3.64 upon the addition of ɩ-carr-CNDs. Typically, the chemistry of MO involves UV–vis red shift with a decrease in pH, which results in the color change from yellow to red. Classically, at pKa = 3.4, the MO changes color, from yellow to red, which modifies the chemical structure (Fig. 4B) increasing the electron delocalization in the MO molecule as the acidity of the solution increases [29]. The characteristic band ascribed to the π→π* transition of the chromophore group of the MO molecule, which absorbs initially at 426 nm, shifts to 523 nm as a result of the n→π* transition of the azo bond [30]. Hence, pH plays a significant role in the observed change in MO color. Since pH is a significant factor, the investigation was conducted to consider the role of acidity in MO interaction. For comparison purposes, the pH of the pure MO solution was deliberately adjusted to 3.57 by adding 0.1 M HCl and was made as the control in determining the effect of CNDs in the chemistry of MO. The pH-adjusted pure MO (pH = 3.57) solution changed color from yellow to bright pink and showed an absorption peak at 523 nm (Fig. 4C) characteristic of the hydrogenation of the azo bond. This peak generally remains the same with no significant decrease in absorbance throughout the 200 min observation time (Table 2 entry 3) indicating that the red hydrogenated MO is stable. Upon dissolution of ɩ-carr CNDs in pure MO solution (without HCl), the immediate color change was observed from yellow to red and the pH was recorded to reduce from 6.32 to 3.64 confirming the acidic nature of CNDs. The absorbances at ∼282 nm due to the CND peak and at 523 nm were observed (Table 2 entry 2) showing that the acidic CNDs promote the same chemistry as that of H+ and that the hydrogenation of the azo bond in MO was facilitated by the CNDs. Monitoring the peak at 523 nm over 200 min showed a decreasing trend (Table 2 entries 4–11) while sustaining the absorbances due to the CNDs (∼282 nm). This indicates that the MO concentration decreases despite maintaining the 3.0 mg/L MO concentration and suggests that the degradation of MO occurs. The decrease in absorbance peak at 523 nm is accompanied by a color change from red to bright pink. The efficiency of ɩ-carr CNDs to degrade MO reached its maximum at t = 170 min and no further degradation was observed beyond that. The calculated degradation efficiency after 170 min was found to be 54%.

The composite film containing CNDs from ɩ-carr (2″ x 2”) was investigated for MO degradation. The distribution of CNDs in the film was assumed to be uniform since the preparation involves constant stirring. The embedded CNDs are released when the films are dissolved in the aqueous solution of 3.0 mg/L MO. The time-dependent absorption monitoring of the degradation of MO showed a change in color from the pure MO yellow to CND-loaded orange solution along with a decrease of pH to 4.76. At t = 0 min, the absorption was observed at 426 nm (Table 2 entry 13), but at t = 30 min the color changed to pink and the absorption peak of MO shifted to 523 nm along with the increase of absorbance (Table 2 entry 14). After some time, a decrease of absorbance was observed indicative of the degradation of MO (Table 2 entry 15–16). The degradation efficiency of MO at t = 90 min in the presence of ɩ-carr-CND film with respect to the pH-adjusted MO was found to be 49%. The acidity of the solution and the presence of surface-active sites in CNDs are the influencing factors for the dye degradation reaction.

The effects of different composite films were compared for the degradation of MO dye. The raw carrageenan film without CNDs was used as a control to determine the effect of the film matrix. The time-dependent UV–vis absorption measurement of MO degradation was observed at 0–35 min. Table 1 (columns 3–7) listed the color and pH of the film-loaded MO solutions measured upon the addition of composite films at t = 0 min. The dissolution of the carrageenan film without CNDs (control) to the MO solution showed yellow color, which is the characteristic color of MO at basic condition (pH 6.81). This indicates that the carrageenan did not instantly affect the chemistry of MO. However, when the composite films containing the ᴋ-, ɩ-, and λ-CNDs were added to the MO solution, the immediate color change was observed similar to the color change when pH of MO was adjusted by adding HCl. This indicates that the acidification of the MO instantly occurred upon the addition of CNDs. The intensity of the pink color was dependent on the resulting pH. In contrast, the film composite containing Gal-CNDs did not change the color of the MO solution and remained yellow. The results confirmed that the CNDs from carrageenan behave differently from the galactose-derived CNDs. The corresponding absorbance spectra at t = 0 min reveal that only the pH-adjusted MO solution and the MO containing films with carr-CNDs showed the maximum absorbance peaks (λ max) at 523 nm. The other solutions containing MO with raw κ-carrageenan film (control) and Gal-CNDs showed a maximum peak (λ max) at 426 nm similar to the MO solution indicating that the raw carrageenan and the CNDs from galactose were not able to affect change in the MO structure. The same general observations were noted after the reactions were observed at t = 35 min. The carrageenan film, therefore, is an ideal matrix in this study because it did not affect the dye under investigation and the observed effects are thus attributed to the presence of CNDs in the carrageenan matrix. Moreover, the Gal-CND did not also affect change in the MO structure confirming the critical role of CND precursors in the property of the CNDs. Galactose has the same sugar structure as that of carrageenan, however, it does not contain the sulfate ester groups. Thus, the sulfate ester groups in the precursor play a critical role in imparting a unique property to the resulting CNDs.

1H NMR analysis confirms the ɩ-carr-CND-catalyzed degradation of MO. The 1H NMR spectrum of pure MO (Fig. 5 A) showed singlet at δ 3.13 ppm attributed to the two methyl groups attached to the nitrogen atom. Signals between 7.0 and 8.0 ppm correspond to the protons of the two aromatic rings. The complete peak assignments for MO are A: δ 7.98 ppm, B: δ 7.90 ppm, C: δ 7.86 ppm, D: δ 6.99 ppm, and E: δ 3.13 ppm (Fig. 5A and inset). The 1H NMR spectrum of the ɩ-carr-CNDs (Fig. 5B) was also shown for comparison. The MO solution with ɩ-carr-CNDs (Fig. 5C and D) showed the disappearance of the singlet at δ 3.13 ppm (two methyl groups) suggesting that the tertiary amine has been degraded. In the low field region, the absence of the characteristic aromatic protons at around δ 7.0–8.0 ppm after the addition of ɩ-carr-CNDs indicates the disappearance of the aromatic derivatives. This could have been facilitated initially by the formation of the quinone ring upon hydration of the azo bond since the change in color to red is the first to be observed at low pH followed by the decrease in the intensity of the color. The loss of color intensity over time can be associated with the degradation of the MO molecule, which would occur when the quinone ring is protonated destroying the aromaticity and affecting the cationic amine that would result in its dissociation from the ring [31].

Comparison of the MO degradation activity of the CNDs in films with the raw CNDs showed the latter having lower activity. At t = 180 min, MO degradation efficiency was 3%, 22%, and 21%, using ᴋ-carr CNDs, ɩ-carr CNDs, and λ-carr CNDs, respectively. In comparison, the degradation of MO dye using carr-CND films (Table 1) at a short time (35 min) afforded higher efficiencies at 42% and 51% for ɩ-carr-CND film and λ-carr-CND film, respectively. This indicates that the CNDs embedded in the composite films performed better degradation efficiency than the raw CNDs. Among the carrageenan CNDs, ᴋ-carr CNDs showed a consistent lack of degradation activity on MO both in solution and in film. The environment of CNDs may play a role in the degradation of MO. The raw CNDs gave a more acidic MO solution compared to when CNDs are embedded in films facilitating the formation of quinone forms of MO. However, the degradation is faster using the CND films due to the efficient interaction of MO into the CND binding sites, which would then facilitate the degradative process. Despite the polymeric nature of the carrageenan film matrix, the film structure does not hamper the adsorption process because the acidic nature of the solution promotes the electron delocalization of MO to afford an iminium cation that undergoes electrostatic interaction with the anionic carrageenan film matrix. The degradation process described herein does not necessitate deliberate exposure to intense and high energy sunlight. Most reports on catalytic degradation activity of CNDs are usually triggered by light [7,32,33], but the current work suggests that photoactivity is not necessary to effect degradation.

Carbon nanodots were prepared from carrageenan (ᴋ-, ɩ- and λ-) using hydrothermal carbonization to afford a colloidal solution of CNDs, separated from larger black hydrochar components. The CNDs obtained have quasi-spherical shapes with nano sizes (TEM: 3.8 nm; DLS 1.0–2.3 nm) and having a luminous green property under UV light. UV–Vis analysis confirmed the light-absorbing properties of the CNDs. EDS and FTIR characterization reveal the incorporation of sulfur functional groups in the CND surface. The CNDs were embedded in a carrageenan film matrix to prevent aggregation and to improve its storage stability affording a brown-colored free-standing film. The composite film is soluble in water and allows for the release of the CNDs while the film matrix serves as the stabilizing agent. DLS analysis showed that the hydrodynamic size of the CNDs was affected by the presence of a polymeric carrageenan matrix, which provided stability to the CNDs as shown by the observed zeta potential. The CNDs showed acidic property as it lowers the pH of water. Exploration of the degradation activity of the film-stabilized CNDs showed better activity towards the degradation of methyl orange (MO) dye compared to the raw CNDs in solution. The degradation activity was only observed when using the carrageenan-derived CNDs since CNDs prepared from galactose did not promote degradation of MO confirming the role of the CND precursors and that the sulfate ester groups in carrageenan play a critical role in imparting a unique property to the resulting CNDs. Among the carrageenans, the CNDs derived from iota-carrageenan showed the most efficient degradation activity. The CNDs imparted acidity to the dyes that protonate the MO and degrade the dye over time. The study demonstrated the influence of the unique chemistry of the carrageenan precursor imparting unique property to CNDs. Moreover, the study demonstrated that the CNDs can be stored in a film matrix that can be used for point-of-use and on-field degradation of dyes. Further exploration is recommended for other potential applications.

Jessa C. Leuterio: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Drexel H. Camacho: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Jose Paolo O. Bantang: Investigation. Cristian Ryan A. Argamino: Software, Supervision.

The DLSU-CIF NMR and Spectroscopy laboratories are acknowledged for the use of instruments. We thank Ms. May Bernardo of UP Manila for her valuable help in the Fluorescence analysis. We wish to acknowledge Virgilio Ebajo, Jr., Jude Rolan dela Cruz, Alona Intac, and Deanne Hazel Pelobello of DLSU-CIF for assistance in conducting the experiments.
